days.title: Arriving at the restaurant

grammar.title: The accusative case: direct objects
grammar.explanation: When a noun is the **direct object** of a verb (the thing you see, want, love), it goes in the **accusative case**. Good news: **inanimate masculine** nouns (like *стол*, *ресторан*) and **neuter** nouns (like *меню*, *кафе*) look exactly the same as the dictionary form. So *Я вижу стол* (I see a table) uses *стол* unchanged. This week we add the endings that actually change.

reading.title: У входа
reading.text: — Добрый вечер! Это свободный стол? — Да. Вот меню, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — Good evening! Is this table free? — Yes. Here is the menu, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine you walk into a café. Greet the waiter and ask if a table is free. Then ask for the menu. Say it aloud in Russian.

days.title: I love this dish (любить + accusative)

grammar.title: Conjugating любить with accusative objects
grammar.explanation: **любить** (to love/like) is a key verb followed by the accusative. It conjugates: *я люблю* (note the extra **л**), *ты любишь*, *он/она любит*, *мы любим*, *вы любите*, *они любят*. The thing loved goes in the accusative. With **inanimate masculine and neuter** nouns the form does not change, so *суп*, *салат*, *сыр*, *хлеб* stay the same: *Я люблю суп*.

reading.title: Что ты любишь?
reading.text: — Что ты любишь? — Я очень люблю суп и хлеб. А ты любишь салат? — Да, люблю.
reading.translation: — What do you like? — I really love soup and bread. And do you like salad? — Yes, I do.

days.speakingPrompt: List three foods you love and one you don't, using любить. For example: Я люблю суп, but try it with сыр and салат too. Say it aloud.

days.title: Ordering a feminine dish (-а → -у)

grammar.title: Feminine nouns in -а: change -а to -у
grammar.explanation: Feminine nouns ending in **-а** change that ending to **-у** in the accusative. So *рыба* → *рыбу*, *пицца* → *пиццу*, *вода* → *воду*, *котлета* → *котлету*, *колбаса* → *колбасу*. This happens whether the noun is animate or not. Use it after verbs like **хотеть** (to want), **заказывать** (to order), and **любить**.

reading.title: Заказ
reading.text: — Что вы хотите? — Я хочу рыбу и воду. — А она заказывает пиццу.
reading.translation: — What would you like? — I want fish and water. — And she is ordering pizza.

days.speakingPrompt: Order a meal: say what you want and what you love using -а → -у feminine nouns (рыба, пицца, вода, котлета). Say it aloud as if talking to a waiter.

days.title: Soft feminine dishes (-я → -ю)

grammar.title: Feminine nouns in -я: change -я to -ю
grammar.explanation: Feminine nouns ending in **-я** change that soft ending to **-ю** in the accusative. So *кухня* → *кухню*, *дыня* → *дыню*, *лазанья* → *лазанью*. This mirrors yesterday's *-а → -у* rule, just with the soft pair of letters. Neuter nouns like *блюдо* and *кафе* do not change.

reading.title: Вкусно!
reading.text: — Я очень люблю эту кухню. Блюдо вкусное! — А я хочу дыню. Она сладкая.
reading.translation: — I really love this cuisine. The dish is tasty! — And I want melon. It's sweet.

days.speakingPrompt: Say which cuisine you love and name one sweet dish you want, using -я → -ю nouns (кухня, дыня). Add вкусный or сладкий to describe it. Say it aloud.

days.title: I see the waiter (animate masculine -а/-я)

vocab.exampleEn: I am waiting for a friend.
I am waiting for the waiter.

grammar.title: Animate masculine nouns: add -а / -я
grammar.explanation: When a **masculine** noun is **animate** (a person or animal), the accusative is NOT the same as the dictionary form. It takes the genitive-style ending: add **-а** to a consonant ending (*официант* → *официанта*, *повар* → *повара*, *друг* → *друга*), or **-я** to a soft ending. Compare with inanimate *стол* → *стол* (no change). So *Я вижу стол* but *Я вижу официанта*.

examples.english: I am waiting for a friend.

reading.title: Где официант?
reading.text: — Я жду официанта. Где он? — Вот он! Я зову его. Дайте счёт, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — I'm waiting for the waiter. Where is he? — There he is! I'm calling him. Give us the bill, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Practice animate masculine accusatives: say you see the waiter, you're waiting for a friend, and you know the chef (официанта, друга, повара). Then ask for the bill. Say it aloud.

days.title: A full order: putting it together

grammar.title: All the accusative endings together
grammar.explanation: Combine this week's rules in one order. **Inanimate masc/neuter** = no change (*суп*, *десерт*, *мороженое*). **Feminine -а → -у** (*курица* → *курицу*, *вилка* → *вилку*). **Feminine -я → -ю** (*кухня* → *кухню*). **Animate masc + -а/-я** (*официант* → *официанта*). Note: feminine animate nouns like *официантка* still follow the feminine rule → *официантку*.

examples.english: I am ordering chicken and dessert.

reading.title: Полный заказ
reading.text: — Я хочу курицу и воду. На десерт — мороженое. — Я зову официантку, она приносит вилку.
reading.translation: — I want chicken and water. For dessert — ice cream. — I'm calling the waitress, she brings a fork.

days.speakingPrompt: Give a complete restaurant order: a main (курицу/рыбу), a drink (воду), and a dessert (мороженое/десерт). Then call the waitress and ask her to bring a fork. Say it all aloud.

days.title: Review: At the restaurant

grammar.title: Accusative recap: the four patterns
grammar.explanation:
This week's accusative in one glance:

1. **Inanimate masculine / neuter** — no change: *стол*, *суп*, *меню*, *мороженое*.
2. **Feminine -а → -у**: *рыба → рыбу*, *вода → воду*, *курица → курицу*.
3. **Feminine -я → -ю**: *кухня → кухню*, *дыня → дыню*.
4. **Animate masculine + -а/-я**: *официант → официанта*, *друг → друга*, *повар → повара*.

Use it after **любить**, **хотеть**, **заказывать**, **видеть**, **ждать**, **знать**, **звать**, **приносить**, **пробовать**.

reading.title: В ресторане
reading.text: — Добрый вечер! Это свободный стол? — Да, вот меню. — Я хочу рыбу и воду, а на десерт — мороженое. — А я люблю курицу. Где официант? — Я зову его. Дайте счёт, пожалуйста.
reading.translation: — Good evening! Is this table free? — Yes, here is the menu. — I want fish and water, and for dessert — ice cream. — And I love chicken. Where is the waiter? — I'm calling him. Give us the bill, please.

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play a full restaurant visit on your own: greet, ask for a free table and the menu, order a main + drink + dessert using correct accusative endings, call the waiter, and ask for the bill. Try to use at least one example of each of the four accusative patterns (inanimate, feminine -у, feminine -ю, animate masculine).
